title: Rounding Almost Commuting Hamiltonians
authors: Islam Faisal, Anand Natarajan, Alexander Poremba
abstract: Commuting Hamiltonians lie at the boundary between classical constraint satisfaction and quantum many-body physics, exhibiting rich quantum structure while remaining more tractable than general noncommuting models. In contrast, physical Hamiltonians are rarely exactly commuting, which naturally motivates the study of almost commuting Hamiltonians. Despite their relevance, the implications of approximate commutation are only poorly understood.   In this work, we show how to efficiently approximate any almost commuting $2$-local qubit Hamiltonian by a commuting one: we give a locality-preserving algorithmic rounding technique that maps any $2$-local Hamiltonian $H=\sum_{i=1}^m h_i$ with $\|[h_i,h_j]\| \leq ε$ to a nearby Hamiltonian $\hat{H}$ whose terms pair-wise commute, and which is within overall distance $\|H-\hat{H}\| = O(m\,ε^{1/6})$. As a consequence, we show that $δ$-approximations to the ground energy for $ε$-almost commuting $2$-local qubit Hamiltonians lie in $\mathsf{NP}$ when $δ\gg mε^{1/6}$, extending the classical containment well beyond the commuting setting. Finally, we present two applications of our rounding framework: Gibbs sampling and fast Hamiltonian simulation for almost commuting systems.
summary: This paper introduces a new algorithm to transform almost-commuting 2-local qubit Hamiltonians into exactly commuting ones with controlled error. This technique allows for the extension of complexity results from the commuting regime to the almost-commuting regime and provides practical speedups for quantum Gibbs sampling and Hamiltonian simulation.
main_problem: Developing a way to efficiently round 2-local qubit Hamiltonians with almost-commuting terms to nearby exactly commuting Hamiltonians while preserving locality.
main_result: The authors provide a deterministic, linear-time algorithm that maps an epsilon-almost commuting 2-local Hamiltonian to a commuting one with an error bound of O(m * epsilon^{1/6}), extending NP-containment results to the almost-commuting regime.
method: A 'gap or snap' algorithmic approach using a pinching superoperator and local pivots to force commutation, combined with a 'snapping' step to handle nearly-degenerate terms.
model_or_system: 2-local qubit Hamiltonians where the commutator of any two local terms is bounded by epsilon.
key_observables: Ground energy approximations, Gibbs state trace distance, and Hamiltonian simulation error.
important_parameters: epsilon (commutator norm), m (number of terms), delta (energy gap/precision), and eta (spectral gap threshold).
main_assumptions: The results are specific to 2-local qubit systems and assume the Hamiltonian is provided as an explicit classical string of local terms.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the locality-preserving transformation on a 2D lattice; Figure 2 depicts a commutation graph for a 3-qubit triangle; Figure 3 illustrates a 3-qubit triangle Hamiltonian example.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of almost-commuting Hamiltonians, presents a locality-preserving rounding algorithm using a gap-or-snap technique, proves error bounds and complexity implications (NP-containment), and demonstrates applications in Gibbs sampling and Hamiltonian simulation.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and quantum algorithms, as it provides a way to treat physically realistic, non-commuting systems using the more tractable framework of commuting Hamiltonians, potentially accelerating simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: The commutation graph of the 3-qubit triangle Hamiltonian. The vertices represent matrices from the local Pauli expansion of each Hamiltonian term; the top and bottom layers indicate the two sets of pos- sible expansions (depending on the qubit), and the edges represent near- commutation across Hamiltonian terms, as in (18), (19) and (20). For each qubit, our rounding procedure selects a pivot with a large spectral gap (e.g., the red vertex) and uses it to pinch all of the relevant vertices belonging to the same qubit (e.g. the green vertices). While vertices belonging to the same cluster are not required to commute, we also choose to pinch within clusters to exploit transitivity:...
